faqs.q: When does grief become prolonged grief disorder?
faqs.a: The DSM-5-TR sets a threshold of at least one year after the death for adults, and six months for children and teens. Beyond timing, the grief has to stay intense and disabling, with persistent yearning or preoccupation that keeps interfering with daily life.

faqs.q: Is prolonged grief disorder the same as depression?
faqs.a: No. They can overlap, but they're distinct. Depression usually spreads a low mood across most of life, while prolonged grief centers on the specific person who died and the longing to have them back.

faqs.q: Can prolonged grief disorder be treated?
faqs.a: Yes. Grief-focused therapy designed for this condition has evidence behind it. A clinician can also check for depression or other conditions that sometimes occur alongside it.
